snapshot.v: Baltimore Business Journal's #1 MD benefits administrator, 23 years running (2002–2024)
Wilmington DE · Rockville MD · Philadelphia · D.C.

leadership.name: The Honorable Francis X. Kelly Jr.
leadership.title: Chairman & Co-Founder
leadership.note: Co-founded the company with his wife Janet in 1976; continues to serve as Chairman.

leadership.name: Frank Kelly III
leadership.title: Chief Executive Officer
leadership.note: CLU, RHU, REBC. President since 1994, CEO since 2007 — past president, Maryland & Baltimore Associations of Health Underwriters.

leadership.name: John Kelly
leadership.title: Chief Innovation & Strategy Officer · President, Strategies & Advisory

leadership.name: David Kelly
leadership.title: Chief Organizational Officer · President, Payroll & Advantage

leadership.name: Bryan Kelly
leadership.title: EVP, Kelly Benefits Advantage
leadership.note: Fourth brother in the family leadership team.

leadership.name: Wesley Mace
leadership.title: Chief Product Officer · COO, Advantage & Payroll

leadership.name: Katherine Vahdani
leadership.title: Chief Technology Officer
leadership.note: Joined April 2026 — leads a 70-person tech team; first non-family C-suite technology hire.

leadership.name: Joshua Freeman
leadership.title: Strategy & Innovation Leader · Chief of Staff to the Chief Innovation & Strategy Officer

leadership.name: David Kelly Jr.
leadership.title: VP, Business Development — Capital Region, Strategies

history.deal: Frank & Janet Kelly launch Francis X. Kelly Associates, Inc.
history.sig: Started from the bedroom of their Maryland home, pooling small companies together through trade associations to find affordable health coverage.

history.deal: Gary Chick, CLU joins — becomes Kelly-Chick & Associates
history.sig: The business moves from the bedroom to the basement.

history.deal: Frank Kelly III, John, David, and Bryan Kelly join full-time
history.sig: All four sons join the family business after college, setting up the leadership structure still in place today.

history.deal: Move to 301 International Circle, Hunt Valley, MD
history.sig: Outgrew its fifth office facility since founding — housed ~85 employees serving ~5,000 corporate clients at the time.
Had been President since 1994; marks the formal generational leadership transition.

history.deal: Kelly Benefits Strategies acquires Jolles Insurance
history.sig: A Howard County, MD brokerage — an early example of the acquisition-driven growth pattern.
Extended Kelly's footprint into PA, DC, DE, NY, NJ, WV, and CT.

history.deal: Katherine Vahdani named Chief Technology Officer
history.sig: First non-family C-suite technology hire, leading a 70-person tech team — a real signal about where the next growth phase is expected to come from.
Half a century from a bedroom startup to ~10,000 corporate clients across four divisions.

divisions.name: Strategies
divisions.desc: Employee benefits consulting and brokerage — the front door of the client relationship. Covers the full benefits lifecycle: consulting, plan design, implementation, and ongoing support across medical, dental, vision, and Medicare options.
divisions.scale: Kelly Benefits Strategies' revenue is estimated around $250M (third-party estimate, private company) — a genuine regional player standing next to firms valued at 20–100× its size.
divisions.caption: Figures: public company revenue (SEC/annual reports) for the national brokers; Kelly Benefits Strategies is a third-party revenue estimate since it's privately held.

divisions.name: Advantage
divisions.desc: Benefits administration and technology — compliance solutions, COBRA & state continuation administration (2% premium admin fee model), a call center for employers and participants, and broker technology licensed out to other brokerages.
divisions.scale: Kelly doesn't disclose Advantage's standalone revenue. Shown here is the competitive field it operates in — TPAs and benefits-admin platforms of very different scales.
divisions.caption: All four figures from the Industry Landscape page — SEC filings for public companies, third-party estimate for privately-held Businessolver.

divisions.name: Payroll
divisions.desc: Full-service payroll administration across all 50 states — pay processing, new hire registry, garnishments, and electronic tax filing — integrated with benefits deductions through the shared Total Benefits Solution® platform.
divisions.scale: ADP alone processes payroll at roughly 80× Kelly's estimated total company scale — payroll is the division where Kelly competes hardest against sheer size.
divisions.caption: ADP and Paychex from SEC filings. Gusto is private and doesn't disclose revenue — shown for competitive context only.

divisions.name: Advisory
divisions.desc: 401(k) and retirement plan consulting and investment advisory. Operates as Global Retirement Partners, LLC dba Kelly Benefits Advisory — an SEC-registered investment advisor. Notably, this is the one division that runs under a larger outside aggregator's regulatory umbrella rather than standing fully independent.
divisions.scale: Measured in assets under management (AUM), not revenue — a different yardstick from the other three divisions, since this is an investment advisory business.

competitors.name: OneDigital
competitors.note: closest model parallel: health + wealth convergence

divisions.caption: AUM figures from firm disclosures / SEC ADV filings as reported in trade press. Nearly 20 similar retirement-advisory aggregators exist — this is a sample, not exhaustive.

win.name: Win
win.desc: New business — getting the client. Not yet confirmed in detail; the name implies sales enablement rather than direct sales.

construct.name: Construct
construct.desc: Confirmed: implementation and integration — building out a new client's setup before it goes live.

funcs.label: EDI / data feed setup for new clients
Testing & QA of new client setups

protect.name: Protect
protect.desc: Believed to include broker management — not yet confirmed. The name suggests a compliance/risk function.

connect.name: Connect
connect.desc: Confirmed: this is your seat — COBRA administration.

serve.name: Serve
serve.desc: Confirmed: dedicated service — the coworkers near your cubicle.

processConnectionsNote: A working model of how work actually moves between Advantage's functions — inferred from the vertical-level workflow and standard TPA operating patterns, not independently confirmed function-by-function. Treat it as a draft to correct against how the work actually happens in your seat.

processConnections.note: Pre-sale data scoping informs the EDI feed build
Underwriting determines who's eligible before the initial load
The initial EDI setup becomes what's monitored on an ongoing basis
The initial load is the baseline reconciliation checks against
Onboarding enrollment setup is what annual open enrollment builds on
Direct billing depends on carrier connections already being live
New plan builds get audited for compliance after configuration
COBRA participant questions route to the call center
Reconciled eligibility data feeds claims processing
Compliance reviews the same plan builds Construct configures
Both touch the same ongoing data feed system

strengths: Integration across all four divisions — one client relationship instead of four vendors
Total Benefits Solution® / KTBSonline is genuinely in-house, not licensed — full control over the product
50 years of family stability and reputation; 23 consecutive years as MD's #1 benefits administrator
Advantage's broker-technology licensing gives it a second revenue line beyond direct clients
weaknesses: Strategies is a regional player next to national giants — Gallagher alone is ~55× its estimated revenue
Advisory isn't fully independent — it runs under Global Retirement Partners/HUB's regulatory umbrella
No public financials — headcount and scale are third-party estimates even for someone working there
Limited brand visibility outside the Mid-Atlantic region
opportunities: Push the health + wealth convergence further — OneDigital is proving the model at scale
Grow Advantage's tech-licensing line as a distinct product, not just a byproduct
Continue the Gettysburg-style acquisition pattern to grow Strategies inorganically
Use the 50th anniversary as a real visibility moment beyond the home region
threats: PE-backed roll-ups (Navia, OneDigital, HUB) have more acquisition capital than a private family firm
Industry consolidation could squeeze independent regional players from both ends
A new CTO and 70-person tech team is a bet — if it underdelivers, competitors' platforms may pull ahead
Client tech expectations keep rising (consumer-grade UX), raising the cost of staying competitive

integrationNote: Why the circle matters — each division individually competes against specialists that are often dramatically larger — Gallagher in consulting, WEX/Alight in administration, ADP/Paychex in payroll, CAPTRUST/OneDigital in retirement. Almost none of those specialists connect all four under one client relationship and one technology platform the way Kelly does. If there's a strategic edge here, it's integration, not any single division's scale — and OneDigital (health + wealth convergence) is the one competitor pursuing the same idea, just at a much larger scale.

crossCutting.enrollment: Enrollment isn't one thing — it likely spans three verticals: initial enrollment setup during onboarding (Construct), COBRA elections specifically (Connect), and ongoing annual open enrollment support (Serve). Worth confirming whether Advantage even splits it that way, or handles it as one continuous function.
crossCutting.reconciliation: Reconciliation (eligibility, billing) most plausibly sits in Connect — it's the same "keep two systems in sync" work as COBRA admin — but could also be a Serve function if it's framed as client-facing account maintenance rather than backend data work.

divisionFlowNote: A working model of how one client relationship moves through all four divisions in sequence — inferred from each division's stated role, not independently confirmed handoff-by-handoff. Treat it as a draft to correct.

divisionFlow.label: New client sold & scoped
divisionFlow.detail: Strategies closes the deal and hands the signed plan design to Advantage, which owns implementation and day-to-day administration from here forward.

divisionFlow.label: Eligibility & deductions sync
divisionFlow.detail: Advantage's enrollment and eligibility data feeds Payroll's deduction processing, so a COBRA election or a new hire's enrollment shows up correctly on the next pay cycle.

divisionFlow.label: Contribution data to plan investment
divisionFlow.detail: Payroll's contribution data flows into Advisory's retirement and investment plan administration for the same client's employees.

divisionFlow.label: Renewal & relationship review
divisionFlow.detail: As the plan year turns over, Advisory's account standing feeds back into Strategies' renewal conversation with the client — closing the loop rather than ending it.
